system.description:
The Ironclad Samurai are the warrior-nobility of Tesshari — the spine of the Caste of Steel and the living embodiment of the Iron Sutra's most demanding ideal. They are not merely soldiers. They are philosophical instruments, shaped over years of combined martial and spiritual discipline into weapons that believe in their own sharpness. Their enhancements are not tools bolted onto a body. They are extensions of self, integrated with tendon and resonance, tuned to the specific rhythm of their Echomind. A samurai who loses their modifications does not become a lesser fighter — they become a different person entirely, incomplete in a way that is difficult to describe and impossible to ignore.

The path to the Ironclad begins in youth, with selection by a lord or the Iron Sutra's military orders. The candidate is stripped of whatever enhancements they arrived with — childhood additions, family modifications, the cheap work of a market Forge Tender — and rebuilt from the substrate. This process is called the Hollowing Threshold, and it is not metaphorical. The candidate is rendered to minimum viable enhancement for a period of weeks, experiencing what the Sutensai call the Thin Place: a state of reduced resonance where the Echomind feels distant and the flesh feels cold. Most candidates emerge from the Hollowing Threshold changed. Some do not emerge at all. Those who pass are rebuilt according to the lord's specification and the samurai order's tradition, new enhancements inscribed with sigils of obligation, their resonant metal threaded through with oaths.

To be an Ironclad Samurai is to live in service to a concept larger than personal survival. The code they follow — the Tesshari equivalent of bushido, called the Vein of Iron — demands not merely loyalty and courage but something more demanding: correctness. Every action must be correct. The correct bow at the correct angle. The correct killing strike, delivered without unnecessary suffering. The correct death, met without flinching. The Between, in Iron Sutra theology, is the resonant space where soul and machine achieve equilibrium — and the Vein of Iron is a set of prescriptions for maintaining that equilibrium under duress. A samurai who abandons the code does not merely become dishonorable. They become spiritually misaligned, their enhancements losing their resonant clarity, their strikes becoming technically correct but spiritually hollow.

The darkness of the Ironclad's existence is this: the Vein of Iron is a perfect trap. It demands absolute service to a lord who may not deserve it. It demands honor in a world that has ceased to honor anything. It demands correct death at a time when death has become either digital immortality in the Iron Afterlife or the horrific dissolution of Shell-state. The Ironclad Samurai know this. The best of them know it clearly and choose the code anyway — not out of naivety but out of a conviction that some things must be maintained precisely because everything else has rotted. The worst of them know it and have found ways to use the code as cover for atrocity, claiming correctness for acts that serve only themselves.
